items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What sport is best suited to type I (slow-twitch) fibres?
items.why: Students confuse which fibres suit which event. Type I = slow, fatigue-resistant, aerobic (endurance). Type II = fast, powerful, anaerobic (sprint/power). Distance runners are type-I-dominant.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.
The answer examiners want is: Net external torque is zero. “Net force is zero” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Pulling arms in during a spin:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Decreases I and increases ω. “Decreases ω and increases I” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A skater with I=4 kg m² spins at ω=2 rad s⁻¹. Pulling arms in halves I. New ω (rad s⁻¹) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
